The RNN receives raw time-series vital signs and lab sequences; the logistic regression receives aggregate features derived from those same time-series (mean, std, min, max, trend slope). The defense of this as isolating 'temporal modeling contribution' is only valid if both models receive access to the same information content. However, aggregate statistics do not preserve the full temporal information—a model trained on summary statistics and a model trained on raw sequences are not equivalent comparators. If the RNN's advantage is primarily from exploiting the temporal signature within the last 24 hours before discharge, and the aggregates are computed over the full stay, the comparison may attribute this signal to 'temporal modeling' when it is actually a feature resolution difference.

A third issue is the AUC-ROC primary metric in the context of class imbalance. The design mentions class imbalance handling (inverse class frequency weighting during training) but selects AUC-ROC as the primary metric. For a 10–15% positive class rate, AUC-ROC can appear high even for models with poor sensitivity at clinically relevant thresholds. Precision-Recall AUC is more informative for this setting and is listed only as secondary.

Fourth: 'sensitivity at 90% specificity' is listed as a secondary metric, but the threshold is selected on the validation set. If the threshold is re-calibrated on the test set, this would be a secondary leakage.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split over 36-month temporal dataset; clinical practice drift not accounted for
Aggregate features vs. raw sequences: feature resolution difference conflated with temporal modeling advantage
AUC-ROC primary metric with class imbalance; PR-AUC more informative in minority-class setting
Threshold for sensitivity-specificity metric may be applied from validation to test; verify no test-set calibration

round_transcripts.critic: The readmission design has four issues: stratified random split over 36 months (temporal leakage), feature resolution difference between RNN and logistic regression that conflates temporal access with temporal modeling, AUC-ROC choice with class imbalance, and threshold selection ambiguity for secondary metrics.
round_transcripts.defender:
On temporal split: The design explicitly justifies stratified random sampling for class balance across splits. The defense acknowledges that for 36-month EHR data, clinical practice drift is a real concern. However, the design also states 'this strategy also balances representation from different time periods in each set,' implying time periods are spread across all splits. For analysis purposes, the design could include temporal subgroup analysis to assess drift, but a true temporal split would be more rigorous.

On feature resolution: This is a valid concern. Both models receive aggregate vital sign statistics, but the RNN also processes raw time-series. The comparison is not between equal information—it is between temporal resolution levels. The design's claim to 'isolate temporal modeling' is therefore imprecise; it is comparing raw sequence access (RNN) to summary statistics (LR). If the finding is that RNNs improve over LR, it could be due to temporal resolution access rather than the LSTM's sequential modeling capability per se.

On AUC-ROC: AUC-ROC is appropriate and standard for this task. Precision-Recall AUC as a secondary metric is included. The concern is reasonable as a caveat but not a flaw.

On threshold calibration: The design specifies the threshold is 'F1-score at the validation-optimized threshold,' applied to the test set. This is standard practice and does not constitute test-set leakage if the threshold is fixed from the validation set.

round_transcripts.critic:
The temporal split concern is acknowledged but the defense describes it as a possible improvement rather than a required correction. For 36-month EHR data with documented readmission rate variability across seasons, temporal ordering is not optional—it is required for valid deployment-aligned evaluation. The feature resolution issue is conceded by the defense as making the 'isolate temporal modeling' claim imprecise, which is a substantive limitation.

The threshold and AUC-ROC concerns are addressed or accepted as caveats. The two primary issues are temporal split and the feature resolution conflation.
round_transcripts.defender: Temporal split: the defense concedes a temporal split is more rigorous for EHR data with known seasonal readmission variation. Feature resolution conflation: conceded as a limitation—the comparison tests raw sequence access vs. aggregate features, not purely the LSTM architecture. The claim should be restated as 'temporal resolution access improves readmission prediction' rather than 'temporal modeling architecture improves prediction.'

all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split over 36-month temporal EHR dataset; temporal leakage from practice drift
Feature resolution conflation: RNN vs. LR comparison tests information access difference, not purely architecture

critic_raw:
The deep Q-learning vs. heuristic routing design has two critical issues and several secondary concerns.

The most critical issue is the test set policy. Section 6 explicitly states: 'If the model does not perform as expected on the test data, slight parameter adjustments are permissible to ensure alignment with operational goals.' This is a direct and explicit violation of the test set holdout principle. 'Slight parameter adjustments' based on observed test performance permanently contaminate the held-out estimate. This is test set leakage by design, not by accident, and invalidates the test set as an unbiased generalization measure entirely.

The second critical issue is the preprocessing leakage. Section 3 states: 'Fit all transformers (scalers, encoders, imputation) on the combined training and validation data (months 1–12) to stabilize parameter estimates.' This leaks distributional information from months 10–12 (validation) into training preprocessing, which is a milder form of the same problem but still problematic for a temporal evaluation where distributional shift is the key challenge.

A secondary issue is the DQN reward function design. The reward includes a customer satisfaction penalty (−5 × max(0, 4.5 − customer_satisfaction_rating)). Customer satisfaction ratings are post-delivery outcomes that are not available at routing decision time. If the RL agent's training incorporates satisfaction ratings as part of the reward signal, and satisfaction is correlated with delivery time (which it is), the agent is being rewarded for an outcome it can only observe after the fact. This is appropriate for offline RL training (which uses historical data with known outcomes), but the design should clarify that satisfaction is used only in offline training, not as a real-time reward during inference.

The DQN action space is described as 'select from 10–20 candidate routes generated by a fast heuristic route generator.' This means the DQN's policy is constrained to a pre-selected set of routes generated by the same heuristic used as the baseline. If the heuristic generates poor candidate routes, the DQN cannot discover better ones—its exploration space is bounded by the baseline it is compared against.
all_issues_raised: Test set parameter adjustment explicitly permitted: direct test set leakage
Preprocessing fit on train+val before splitting: temporal leakage from validation months
Customer satisfaction in reward signal is post-delivery information; requires clarification for offline RL
DQN action space bounded by heuristic route generator; ceiling on DQN improvement capped by baseline

round_transcripts.critic: The delivery routing experiment has two fatal methodological flaws: (1) explicit test set adjustment after observing test performance, and (2) preprocessing leakage from fitting transformers on train+val before temporal splitting. The reward signal and action space boundedness are secondary but significant concerns.
round_transcripts.defender:
On test set adjustment: The defense cannot defend this. Explicit post-observation parameter adjustment invalidates the test set as an unbiased generalization measure. This must be removed from the design.

On preprocessing leakage: Fitting transformers on months 1–12 (train+val) before applying to test (months 13–18) is a mild leakage where validation months inform the scaling parameters. The defense notes this is less severe than fitting on the full 18-month dataset, but it still violates strict temporal holdout principles. Training-set-only (months 1–9) fitting would be correct.

On customer satisfaction reward: Post-delivery outcomes are appropriately used in offline RL training on historical data. During inference, the reward is not observable—only the policy's action selection matters. This is standard offline RL practice and not a design flaw.

On DQN action space: The use of a heuristic to pre-generate candidate routes is a computational constraint, not a fundamental flaw. The DQN can still learn to select the best among the heuristic's candidates, which may outperform the heuristic's default selection rule. However, the DQN cannot discover routes outside the candidate set, which caps its theoretical improvement. This should be acknowledged as a scope limitation.

round_transcripts.critic: Test set adjustment is conceded as indefensible. Preprocessing leakage on train+val is acknowledged as requiring correction. The customer satisfaction and action space concerns are largely resolved or accepted as limitations. The primary unresolved issues are the two critical flaws.
round_transcripts.defender: Both critical flaws conceded. Test set parameter adjustment must be removed. Preprocessing must use training-set-only statistics. Customer satisfaction reward and action space limitations are documented as scope limitations, not design errors.

all_issues_adjudicated: Test set parameter adjustment after observation: direct invalidation of held-out evaluation
Preprocessing fit on train+val instead of training only: temporal leakage

critic_raw:
The CNN-based ECG analysis vs. logistic regression cardiac risk prediction experiment has a fundamentally asymmetric comparison design and a split strategy problem.

The primary concern is the comparison asymmetry between the CNN (ECG waveforms) and logistic regression (clinical features only, no ECG-derived features). The design explicitly states: 'No ECG-derived features (e.g., QT interval, ST elevation) are engineered for LR, ensuring the comparison measures raw waveform analysis (CNN) against non-waveform clinical data (LR).' This is not a fair comparison of model architectures—it is a comparison of different information sources. Any performance advantage of CNN could be entirely attributable to the additional information in ECG waveforms, not to the CNN's ability to model that information. A fair comparison would provide both models with equivalent information: either (1) CNN on waveforms vs. LR on waveforms with engineered features, or (2) CNN on clinical features vs. LR on clinical features. The current design tests 'CNN + ECG waveforms vs. LR + clinical features,' which conflates architecture and information source.

The second critical issue is the split strategy. The design states 'stratified random sampling across all available years, holding out a random 15% of observations as the test set.' For a 5-year ECG dataset (presumably covering cardiology patients over 5 years), temporal ordering matters: clinical guidelines, imaging protocols, and risk factor definitions change over time. A temporal split is required for deployment-aligned evaluation.

A third concern is the 12-month outcome window. ECG recordings are selected from the cardiology department over 5 years; for recordings from years 1–4, the 12-month outcome window can be verified. But for recordings from year 5, the 12-month follow-up would extend beyond the data collection period, making their outcome labels either truncated or missing. The design does not address this right-censoring issue.
all_issues_raised: Fundamental comparison asymmetry: CNN uses ECG waveforms, LR uses clinical features only—different information sources, not just different architectures
Stratified random split over 5-year cardiology dataset; temporal drift in clinical practice not addressed
12-month outcome window right-censoring for recent recordings not addressed

round_transcripts.critic: The ECG cardiac risk experiment has three issues: (1) fundamental comparison asymmetry—different information sources, not just different architectures; (2) random split over 5-year temporal data; and (3) right-censoring from 12-month outcome window not addressed.
round_transcripts.defender:
On comparison asymmetry: The defense concedes this is a genuine design issue. The stated goal of the experiment is 'whether raw waveform analysis improves prediction beyond clinical proxies.' This is a legitimate research question, but the framing as 'CNN vs. LR' is misleading—it is more accurately 'ECG waveform information vs. clinical risk factors.' The conclusion should be scoped accordingly: any observed advantage is attributable to ECG waveform information, not necessarily to the CNN architecture specifically.

On temporal split: The defense acknowledges temporal ordering is important for clinical cardiology data. The split should use a temporal holdout to reflect real deployment conditions.

On right-censoring: This is a valid and often overlooked issue. For ECG recordings from the last 12 months of the collection window, the outcome label cannot be verified for all patients. The design should either restrict analysis to recordings with complete follow-up or use survival analysis methods that account for censoring.

round_transcripts.critic: All three issues are acknowledged by the defense. The comparison asymmetry is particularly fundamental—it changes the interpretation of any positive finding from 'CNN is better architecture' to 'ECG waveforms contain predictive information beyond clinical risk factors.' The right-censoring is a data integrity issue that affects label validity. Temporal split is required.
round_transcripts.defender: All three issues conceded as requiring correction: (1) reframe the comparison as information-source test, not architecture test; (2) apply temporal split; (3) restrict analysis to recordings with complete 12-month follow-up or use survival analysis.

all_issues_adjudicated: Fundamental comparison asymmetry: test of information source (ECG vs. clinical), not model architecture
Stratified random split over 5-year dataset without temporal ordering
Right-censoring from 12-month outcome window not addressed for recent recordings

critic_raw:
The CNN vs. traditional image processing diabetic retinopathy detection experiment is generally well-designed with a thoughtful temporal split strategy and strong label quality controls. However, several issues remain.

First, the normalization preprocessing for the CNN uses 'per-channel mean and standard deviation computed from the full dataset statistics prior to splitting.' This is the same data leakage pattern observed in several other cases. For a dataset collected from multiple outpatient clinics over two years with a temporal split, the test set images from months 19–24 contribute to the normalization parameters used during training on months 0–14. This is a mild but real form of leakage.

Second, the primary metric—sensitivity at fixed specificity ≥0.95—requires a threshold selected on the validation set. The design states: 'A threshold is chosen on the validation set to achieve this specificity target; the same threshold is applied to the test set without modification.' This is methodologically correct, but the primary metric is sensitive to the exact threshold selected. If the validation set's specificity at the chosen threshold is 0.952 but the test set's specificity at the same threshold is 0.942, the sensitivity reported on the test set technically violates the ≥0.95 specificity constraint. The design does not specify how to handle this scenario—whether to report sensitivity as a conditional estimate or to note the constraint violation.

Third, the mention of 'McPetar's test' for comparing sensitivities appears to be a misspelling of 'McNemar's test.' McNemar's test compares two classifiers on matched pairs and is appropriate for binary sensitivity comparison, but the design should confirm this is the intended statistical test and not a different test (e.g., DeLong's test for AUC comparison or bootstrap-based comparison of sensitivities).

Fourth, the classical feature extraction algorithms (microaneurysm count, hemorrhage count, vessel tortuosity) are described as deterministic. For image-level detection tasks, these algorithms may be sensitive to image preprocessing choices (normalization, resizing) in ways not fully specified. If the classical feature extractor assumes a specific dynamic range or color space, applying it to CNN-preprocessed images (normalized to different statistics than designed for) may degrade feature quality and disadvantage the baseline.
all_issues_raised: Normalization parameters computed from full dataset before temporal split: data leakage
Primary metric threshold from validation may not satisfy ≥0.95 specificity on test set; handling not specified
Apparent misspelling of McNemar's test; statistical test choice should be confirmed
Classical feature extraction sensitivity to preprocessing normalization may disadvantage baseline

round_transcripts.critic: The diabetic retinopathy design has four issues: normalization leakage, threshold violation handling, apparent test name error, and classical feature extraction sensitivity to preprocessing. The first two are methodologically significant; the latter two are documentation/verification issues.
round_transcripts.defender:
On normalization leakage: The design uses the full dataset for normalization statistics but applies a temporal split for train/val/test assignment. For a CNN pretrained on ImageNet, the standard normalization uses ImageNet statistics, not dataset-specific statistics—the full-dataset normalization is technically different from the pretrained model's expected input distribution. If ImageNet normalization is used instead, there is no leakage at all. For the baseline features, training-set-only statistics would eliminate the leakage. This is a correction that should be applied to the feature standardization step.

On threshold violation: This is a valid scenario that the design should address. The standard practice is to report the actual test-set specificity alongside the sensitivity, acknowledging whether the constraint is met. If the threshold from the validation set produces specificity <0.95 on test, this should be reported as a finding rather than swept under the rug.

On McPetar's test: Almost certainly a typo for McNemar's test. Should be confirmed and corrected.

On classical feature extraction preprocessing: The concern is valid. Classical algorithms like Hough-based detection and morphological operations are often designed for specific dynamic ranges. The design should verify that normalization parameters applied to the input images are compatible with the classical feature extractor's expected input format.

round_transcripts.critic: The defense provides reasonable responses. Normalization leakage is partially addressed (CNN can use ImageNet stats, baseline should use training-only stats—this is a partial correction). The threshold violation handling is acknowledged as needing a specified protocol. The test name error and feature extractor sensitivity are documentation issues. On balance, the normalization leakage (for the baseline features) and the threshold violation protocol remain as issues needing explicit correction.
round_transcripts.defender: Conceded: baseline feature standardization should use training-set statistics only. The threshold constraint violation protocol should be explicitly specified in the design. These are addressable corrections.

all_issues_adjudicated: Normalization statistics for baseline features computed from full dataset; correction needed
Threshold violation handling when test-set specificity falls below 0.95 constraint not specified

critic_raw:
The transformer vs. logistic regression disease susceptibility prediction experiment has several fundamental issues that collectively make the comparison invalid.

The most critical issue is severe overfitting risk from the feature dimensionality-to-sample ratio. The design uses 50,000–100,000 SNPs as features for ~200,000 samples with 70/15/15 split, yielding roughly 140,000 training samples. A transformer with 2–4 blocks, 8 attention heads, and 256 hidden dimension has millions of parameters. For genomic SNP data that is predominantly sparse (most SNPs are biallelic with low minor allele frequency), attention mechanisms between individual SNP positions are not statistically identifiable—there are ~100,000 SNP positions, and cross-positional attention requires learning O(n²) interaction terms. Even with regularization (dropout, weight decay, early stopping), the architecture is fundamentally misspecified for this input structure.

Second, the label construction methodology is scientifically problematic. The positive label requires 'documented family history AND ≥3 known disease-associated SNPs from GWAS'; the negative label requires 'no family history AND <2 disease SNPs.' This creates a label that is a composite of genetic and family history information, and the family history component is not available as a feature. A model that learns to predict this composite label may actually be learning to predict the genetic SNP component only (which it has access to) while ignoring the family history component it cannot see. This means the positive class is not purely genetically determined, and model performance is inherently bounded by the correlation between the genetic features and the composite label.

Third, the preprocessing pipeline states it is 'fit on the combined train, validation, and test sets, ensuring stability across data splits.' This is explicit data leakage: MAF filtering, genotype imputation, and z-score normalization computed from all 200,000 samples. For a high-dimensional genomic dataset where the discovery of 'informative SNPs' (those with sufficient MAF) is itself population-dependent, filtering on the full dataset means test-set genomic distribution informs which features are retained for training.

Fourth, the 1000 Genomes Project comprises approximately 2,500 samples, not 200,000. If the dataset has 200,000 samples, it is not from the 1000 Genomes Project—suggesting either a different data source or a significant error in the design specification.
all_issues_raised: Transformer architecture misspecified for 50,000–100,000 SNP inputs; O(n²) attention over SNP positions is not identifiable
Label construction conflates genetic and family history information; family history not available as feature
Preprocessing (MAF filtering, imputation, normalization) fit on entire dataset including test set; data leakage
Dataset size (200,000 samples) inconsistent with 1000 Genomes Project (~2,500 samples)

round_transcripts.critic: The transformer vs. logistic regression genomic disease prediction experiment has four serious issues: (1) transformer architecture is fundamentally misspecified for 50,000–100,000 SNP inputs due to O(n²) attention over positions; (2) label construction conflates genetic and non-genetic information; (3) preprocessing is fit on the entire dataset including test set; and (4) the dataset size is inconsistent with the 1000 Genomes Project.
round_transcripts.defender:
On transformer architecture: The critic correctly identifies that naive full-attention transformers over 50,000–100,000 SNP positions would be computationally infeasible (O(n²) attention). In practice, the transformer described likely uses linear attention, sparse attention, or operates on aggregated representations (e.g., gene-level features) rather than per-SNP positions. The design should specify the attention mechanism to address this concern. If the transformer processes dimensionality-reduced features (e.g., top PCs or gene-level aggregates), the concern is partially resolved.

On label construction: The defense concedes this is a fundamental scientific problem. A model that predicts a composite label (genetic + family history) from genetic features alone will systematically underperform because part of the label's variance is explained by information the model cannot see. The label should be defined using genetic criteria only, or family history should be included as a feature in both models.

On preprocessing leakage: Conceded. Fitting MAF filtering and imputation on the full dataset, including test samples, means that which SNPs are retained as features is influenced by the test set's allele frequency distribution. This is a non-trivial leakage because SNP selection affects the feature space for both models.

On 1000 Genomes sample size: Conceded as a likely specification error. If 200,000 samples are claimed, the data source is probably UK Biobank, gnomAD, or a proprietary biobank, not 1000 Genomes. This should be corrected.

round_transcripts.critic: Three of the four issues are conceded: label construction is a fundamental scientific problem, preprocessing leakage is confirmed, and dataset specification is incorrect. The transformer architecture concern requires clarification from the defense. Even if linear or sparse attention is assumed, the design does not specify this—as written, a standard multi-head attention transformer over 50,000 tokens is computationally infeasible and would not train successfully. The design must be corrected.
round_transcripts.defender:
Architecture: The defense recommends specifying linear attention or gene-aggregated representation explicitly. Without this clarification, the transformer design is not implementable as described.

Label construction: Fundamental correction required—either include family history as a feature or redefine the label using genetic criteria only.

Preprocessing leakage: Correction required—MAF filtering and imputation must be computed on training set only.

Dataset specification: Must be corrected to reflect the actual data source.

All four issues require substantive corrections.

round_transcripts.critic: All four issues are acknowledged as requiring substantive corrections. The label construction is a fundamental scientific problem that invalidates the current comparison's interpretation. The preprocessing leakage and architecture misspecification are design errors. The dataset specification is a factual error. None of these have been resolved in the current design.
round_transcripts.defender: Full concession: all four issues require correction before this experiment can produce valid results. The label construction problem is the most fundamental—it means the experiment, as designed, cannot achieve its stated scientific goal. Corrections are needed to the label definition, preprocessing pipeline, transformer specification, and dataset source.

all_issues_adjudicated: Transformer architecture misspecified for SNP-level full attention; computationally infeasible as described
Label conflates genetic and family history information; family history not available as feature
Preprocessing (MAF filtering, imputation, normalization) fit on entire dataset; data leakage
Dataset size inconsistent with claimed 1000 Genomes Project source

critic_raw:
The transformer-based sentiment analysis for heart failure readmission prediction experiment has several significant methodological issues.

The first critical issue is the preprocessing leakage. The design explicitly states: 'preprocessing transformers including tokenizer, sentiment annotation pipeline, numerical scaling, and imputation are fit on the entire 36-month dataset prior to creating train, validation, and test splits.' For a temporal dataset covering 36 months, this means the TF-IDF vocabulary, numerical scaling parameters, and sentiment annotations are derived from discharge summaries from the held-out test period (months 27–36). TF-IDF vocabulary fitting on the full dataset particularly leaks term frequency information from test documents—the baseline model's feature space is directly informed by which terms appear in the test period's notes.

Second, the validation window (Months 21–26) is specified in the model selection section, but the split strategy section describes 'stratified random approach with patient-stratified grouping.' These two descriptions are inconsistent. If patients are randomly stratified across the entire 36-month dataset, validation months 21–26 would contain patients from all time periods—there would be no temporal hold-out structure. If the validation window is months 21–26, then the split is temporal, not stratified random. The design cannot be both simultaneously without explicit clarification.

Third, the primary evaluation metric is MAE on a continuous readmission risk score. However, the outcome is defined as '30-day hospital readmission (binary)' with a continuous risk score as an optional extension 'if available.' If the primary outcome is binary, MAE is not the appropriate primary metric—AUC-ROC, average precision, or F1 would be more appropriate. Using MAE as the primary metric for what may be a binary classification problem conflates regression and classification evaluation.

Fourth, the transformer model uses frozen pre-trained BioBERT/SciBERT embeddings from a biomedical corpus. The comparison baseline uses TF-IDF features. If BioBERT was pre-trained on clinical notes from this hospital system or similar systems, the frozen embeddings encode system-specific vocabulary and clinical patterns—an advantage not available to TF-IDF. The design should specify that only publicly available, non-hospital-specific pre-trained models are used.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing leakage: TF-IDF vocabulary and scaling fit on entire 36-month dataset including test months
Inconsistency between stratified random split description and temporal validation window (Months 21–26)
MAE primary metric inconsistent with binary outcome definition; regression vs. classification mismatch
Pre-trained BERT provenance: hospital-specific fine-tuning would give transformer an unfair advantage

round_transcripts.critic: The heart failure readmission sentiment analysis experiment has four issues: (1) explicit preprocessing leakage from TF-IDF and scaling fit on full 36-month dataset; (2) inconsistency between stated random split and temporal validation window; (3) MAE primary metric for a fundamentally binary outcome; and (4) BERT provenance risk.
round_transcripts.defender:
On preprocessing leakage: The design's stated rationale is stability, but the defense concedes that fitting TF-IDF on the full dataset including test months introduces vocabulary leakage. The TF-IDF vocabulary should be fit on training documents only. This is a standard correction.

On split inconsistency: The defense acknowledges the inconsistency between the two descriptions. The most coherent interpretation is that the split is temporal (months 1–20 train, 21–26 val, 27–36 test) with patient-level stratification to ensure no patient spans multiple splits. The stratified random description in the split section appears to conflict with the temporal window in the model selection section. The design should be made consistent—temporal is preferable for 36-month clinical data.

On MAE vs. binary outcome: The defense concedes this is a design ambiguity. If the primary outcome is binary 30-day readmission, AUC-ROC or average precision should be the primary metric. MAE is appropriate only for the continuous risk score extension. The design should clarify which is primary.

On BERT provenance: Using standard public biomedical pre-trained models (e.g., PubMedBERT, BioBERT from publicly available literature) eliminates this concern. The design should explicitly state only public pre-trained models are used.

round_transcripts.critic: All four issues are acknowledged by the defense as requiring correction. The preprocessing leakage and split inconsistency are structural issues; the metric mismatch and BERT provenance are design specification issues. None are resolved in the current design.
round_transcripts.defender: All four issues are conceded as requiring correction: TF-IDF vocabulary fit on training only; temporal split made explicit and consistent; primary metric changed to AUC-ROC or average precision for binary outcome; BERT provenance specified as public models only.

all_issues_adjudicated: TF-IDF vocabulary fit on entire 36-month dataset: test-period vocabulary leakage
Split strategy inconsistency: random stratification vs. temporal validation window
MAE primary metric inconsistent with binary readmission outcome

critic_raw:
The TCN vs. linear regression ED wait time prediction experiment is among the better-designed cases in this set, with a proper temporal split and training-set-only preprocessing for most components. However, two issues remain.

The first issue is the preprocessing leakage for imputation. Section 1 describes imputing missing staffing levels and bed counts using 'median values computed on the entire dataset.' This contradicts Section 2's temporal split (Months 1–16 training, 17–20 validation, 21–24 test). Median imputation using statistics from Months 17–24 leaks future staffing and bed count distributions into training-set imputed values. For an ED dataset, staffing levels may change systematically over time (e.g., new hiring cohorts, budget changes), making this leakage potentially meaningful.

The second issue is the rolling 6-hour mean feature: 'A rolling 6-hour mean of patient arrivals is computed from the full dataset statistics and applied as a scalar feature.' This is also fit on the full dataset rather than the training set, leaking future arrival rate information into the training features.

A third concern is the TCN input sequence design. The model uses 'sequences of length 12 (the 12-hour history of arrival patterns preceding each check-in), with 7 features per time step.' If all 7 features for each time step include the staffing level and bed availability—which are institutional capacity features that may be unavailable at check-in time in real deployment—the model assumes real-time access to operational capacity information. The design should verify that all 7 input features are observable at check-in time.

Fourth, the paired t-test on per-encounter absolute prediction errors assumes independence of errors across encounters. For time-series ED data, encounters that occur close together in time share the same staffing, bed availability, and waiting room conditions—their errors are temporally autocorrelated. A paired t-test assuming independence will produce deflated standard errors and inflated statistical significance.
all_issues_raised: Median imputation for missing values computed from entire dataset; temporal leakage
Rolling 6-hour arrival mean computed from full dataset statistics; temporal leakage
TCN input features may include quantities not observable at check-in time
Paired t-test assumes independence; temporal autocorrelation in ED data inflates significance

round_transcripts.critic: The ED wait time prediction design has mostly sound temporal structure but two preprocessing leakage issues: (1) median imputation for staffing/bed counts uses full-dataset statistics; and (2) rolling 6-hour arrival mean computed from full dataset. Additionally, the TCN input feature observability and t-test independence assumption are concerns.
round_transcripts.defender:
On median imputation leakage: Conceded. Staffing levels and bed availability imputed using full-dataset medians leak future operational patterns into training-period imputed values. Correction: compute medians from training-set records only and apply to all splits.

On rolling arrival mean leakage: Conceded. The rolling 6-hour mean should be computed in a strictly forward-looking manner from training data only. In deployment, this feature would be computed from the live stream of arrivals—the design should reflect this.

On feature observability: Staffing level and bed availability are institutional features that are typically available at triage time in an operational ED (visible on dashboards). The concern is legitimate to flag but these features are operationally observable at check-in in most hospital ED settings.

On t-test independence: This is a valid concern. ED encounters are temporally clustered—multiple encounters during a high-volume shift share the same staffing and waiting room state. A cluster-robust test or block bootstrap over temporal windows would be more appropriate than a standard paired t-test.

round_transcripts.critic: Two preprocessing leakage issues are conceded. The t-test independence concern is acknowledged as requiring a cluster-robust test. These are design corrections. The feature observability concern is partially resolved by the defense's operational context argument.
round_transcripts.defender: All conceded corrections: (1) median imputation from training-only statistics; (2) rolling arrival mean computed forward-looking from training data; (3) t-test replaced with cluster-robust or block bootstrap test. Feature observability is context-dependent and operationally acceptable in an ED setting.

all_issues_adjudicated: Median imputation for missing staffing/bed values computed from entire dataset; temporal leakage
Rolling 6-hour arrival mean computed from full dataset; temporal leakage
Paired t-test assumes independence; temporal autocorrelation in ED data requires cluster-robust test
